text: Transformers are neural network architectures that use self-attention to process sequences in parallel. Unlike RNNs they don't process tokens sequentially, which makes them much faster to train on long sequences.

Self-attention computes a weighted sum of all positions in a sequence. Each position attends to every other position via query, key, and value projections, and the attention weights determine how much each other position contributes.

HNSW stands for Hierarchical Navigable Small World. It is a graph-based approximate nearest neighbor algorithm that builds a multi-layer graph and performs greedy beam search from top layer down to the bottom layer.

BM25 is a sparse lexical retrieval algorithm that scores documents based on term frequency and inverse document frequency, with saturation and length normalization. It is still a strong baseline for keyword-heavy queries.

Cross-encoder rerankers score query and document jointly through a transformer, producing a single relevance score per pair. They are slower than bi-encoders but more accurate, so they are used as a second stage on top-K candidates.

Reciprocal Rank Fusion merges results from multiple retrievers by summing one over rank plus a constant K. It is robust to differently-scaled scores from dense and sparse retrievers and is the default fusion method in production hybrid search.

Sentence embeddings map variable-length text to fixed-dimensional vectors in a semantic space, where cosine similarity reflects meaning similarity. They power semantic search, retrieval-augmented generation, and clustering.
Vector databases store high-dimensional embeddings and serve approximate nearest neighbor queries with low latency. Popular implementations include FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, and pgvector.

Retrieval-augmented generation grounds a language model in external documents by retrieving relevant chunks at query time and stuffing them into the prompt. This reduces hallucination and gives the model access to facts not in its training set.
Chunking splits long documents into smaller passages for embedding. Naive fixed-size chunks can bisect ideas; semantic chunking respects sentence and paragraph boundaries to keep each chunk coherent.

Inverted file index uses k-means clustering to partition vectors into nlist buckets. Queries are routed to the nprobe nearest centroid clusters, trading recall for speed. IVF is what FAISS uses by default at scale.

Product quantization compresses vectors by splitting them into subvectors and quantizing each subvector independently. PQ codes are typically 8 bytes for a 768-dim float vector, a 384x compression with modest recall loss.

ColBERT is a late-interaction retrieval model that computes per-token embeddings for queries and documents, then scores via maxsim over token pairs. It captures fine-grained relevance better than single-vector dense retrieval.

SPLADE produces sparse lexical representations from a transformer, with each dimension corresponding to a vocabulary token. It bridges dense and sparse retrieval and works well with traditional inverted indexes.

Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring their magnitudes. For L2-normalized vectors, cosine similarity equals the dot product, which is why retrieval libraries normalize embeddings before indexing.
FastAPI is a modern Python web framework for building APIs with async support, automatic OpenAPI docs, and Pydantic-based request validation. It is built on Starlette and Uvicorn.
Redis is an in-memory key-value store often used as a cache or session store. In retrieval systems it caches frequent query results to avoid recomputing embeddings and re-running the index search.
Prometheus is a time-series monitoring system that scrapes metrics from instrumented services over HTTP. It is the de facto standard for observability in Kubernetes deployments.

Docker packages applications with their dependencies into portable containers. Container images can be built from a Dockerfile and run identically across development, staging, and production environments.

Kubernetes orchestrates containerized applications across a cluster, handling scheduling, scaling, service discovery, and rolling deployments. It is the dominant platform for production microservice deployments.

Locust is a Python-based load testing tool where you define user behavior in code. It scales to thousands of concurrent simulated users from a single machine and is commonly used to benchmark HTTP APIs.
Mean Reciprocal Rank is a ranking quality metric that averages 1 over the rank of the first relevant result across a query set. It captures how quickly users find what they want.

Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain weighs gains by inverse log of position and normalizes by the ideal ranking, producing a value between 0 and 1. NDCG handles graded relevance better than recall or precision.

Recall measures the fraction of relevant items retrieved over all relevant items in the corpus. In approximate nearest neighbor systems, recall is typically benchmarked against a brute-force flat index as ground truth.
Latency percentiles like p50, p95, and p99 describe tail behavior of a system. Production search systems usually optimize for p99 below 50 milliseconds to keep user experience snappy under load.
